assignment_description:
⍞ Materials: The Art of Asking Better Questions, Journal or Notebook, Bible

➜ READ
Intro-Ch.1, p.3-25

➜ REFLECT
Think about the individuals that you interact with on a daily basis. Identify someone that askes good questions and ask that person what motivstes him or her to ask good questions..

⍞ Materials: The Art of Asking Better Questions, Journal or Notebook, Bible

➜ READ
Ch. 2, p.26-39

➜ REVIEW & LIST
Review the italicized questions throughout this chapter and list three questions in your journal or notebookthat were intriguing to you. Explain why.

⍞ Materials: The Art of Asking Better Questions, Journal or Notebook, Bible

➜ READ
Ch. 3, p.40-57

➜ LISTEN & Track
Divide a piece of paper into four squares. Write one of the four levels of questions in each square. One day this week, listen for the four levels of questions being asked by those around you and track how many times you hear the four levels of questions throughout the day.

⍞ Materials: The Art of Asking Better Questions, Journal or Notebook, Bible

➜ READ
Ch. 4, p.61-71

➜ INTERVIEW
Identify one adult and one person your age to interview using David Brooks' questions listed on p.69. Give yourself plenty of time to interview each person. The goal is to get to know the person better, not simply to get through the questions.

⍞ Materials: The Art of Asking Better Questions, Journal or Notebook, Bible

➜ READ
Ch.5-6, p.72-92

➜ WRITE
In your own words, list and explain the six reasons Jesus asked questions.

After you read ch.5-6, Review Resource 4: Fascinating Facts About The Questions Jesus Asked on p.181-185.

⍞ Materials: The Art of Asking Better Questions, Journal or Notebook, Bible

➜ READ
Ch.7, p.93-101

➜ REFLECT & ASK
Reflect on the questions you have regarding God, the Bible, and faith. Ask the members of your family what questions they have regarding God, the Bible, and faith. Identify the questions that you have in common and as a family, take steps towards answering those questions.

⍞ Materials: The Art of Asking Better Questions, Journal or Notebook, Bible

➜ READ
Ch.8-9, p.105-123

➜ INTERVIEW
Identify a person who is responsible for leading people and schedule a time to interview that person. Identify questions from ch. 8-9 (especially from p, 111 & 116) to ask during the interview. Reflect on your own leadership influence and ask yourself the questions on p. 111 & 116. If time permits, interview several leaders and compare their responses.

⍞ Materials: The Art of Asking Better Questions, Journal or Notebook, Bible

➜ READ
Ch.10, p.126-137

➜ PLAN & IMPLEMENT
Designate a journal to be your Questions Journal. Reflect on the nine practices from ch. 9 and organize the journal accordingly. Do not be tempted to rush this assignment. The more time you spend creating a Questions Journal, the great likelihood you will see a transformation in how you ask questions.

⍞ Materials: The Art of Asking Better Questions, Journal or Notebook, Bible

➜ READ
Ch.11, p. 138-150

➜ PRACTICE
Identify a family member or friend to participate in a question-asking exercise with you. On p.147, read #6 "Hold a questions-only conversation with others." Once you both understand the exercise, initiate the conversation and see how long the discussion lasts. Keep practicing until the exercise becomes more natural.

⍞ Materials: The Art of Asking Better Questions, Journal or Notebook, Bible

➜ READ
Ch.12-Conclusion, p.151-160

➜ RECEIVE FEEDBACK
Identify two people that you interact with consistently and ask them to provide feedback on how you ask questions. Use the questions on p.152-153 to guide the conversation. The purpose of this exercise is to develop the skill of asking better questions. Do not be offended by the feedback you recieve. Embrace the feedback and develop action steps that will help you further develop the ability to ask great questions.

⍞ Materials: The Art of Asking Better Questions, Journal or Notebook, Bible

➜ READ
Resource 2: Questions To Ask Ourselves, p.171-173

➜ REFLECT & REVIEW
Review the Self-Reflection Questions on p.171-173. Which questions are the most difficult to answer? What changes do you want to make in light of these questions.

⍞ Materials: The Art of Asking Better Questions

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to course.
assignment_teachersNote:
★ Econ/Civics: Grade 11 (U.S.) — TERM 1 EXAM QUESTIONS
· The Art of Asking Better Questions: Explain how curiosity aids in developing the skill of asking great questions.

· The Art of Asking Better Questions: If a friend asked you, "Why is it important to develop the skill of asking better questions?" how would you reply?

assignment_description:
⍞ Materials: What Would Keynes Do?, link, and notebook

➜ NOTE
Part 2 lessons cover Ch.5, sections of Ch.3, and Ch.4.

➜INTRO
Skim back over the sections to remind yourself of the topics from Part 1; remember that you encountered several economists and their approaches/positions on the specific issue being covered. Focus on theory and positions rather than trying to memorize names.

• DEFINITIONS:
The marginal tax rate is the amount of additional tax paid for every additional dollar earned as income. The average tax rate is the total tax paid divided by the total income earned. If you want to know more, see the expanded definition from TaxEDU.

➜ VIEW
∞ Link: Marginal Tax Rate (TaxEDU)

➜ RECAP
Opportunity cost = the next best alternative you forgo (p.45)

Deadweight welfare = money spent is greater than utility gained, leading to an inefficient allocation of resources (p.71)

Income effect = higher wages mean we can gain our target income through working less and enjoying more leisure time (p.93)

➜ READ
p.150-157

➜ DISCUSS and/or WRITE
• "There is no such thing as a tax cut without an opportunity cost" (p.153). If discussing these lessons with someone is not an option, write down your thoughts about the quote.

TEACHER TIP:
Discussion of these lessons adds a lot to the study of economics.

• COMPOSITION
Write an essay on the economic positions of "sweatshops." Be sure to include your position and support your answer with the text and/or other sources.

⍞ Materials: What Would Keynes Do?, notebook

• DEFINITIONS:
A subsidy is a direct or indirect payment by a government to help an industry or business, or to aid individuals and households. Economies of scale refer to the cost reduction when a company increases production, thus spreading the cost over a broader area.

➜ READ
p.158-164

➜ DISCUSS and/or WRITE
• Discuss Noma's "nuanced" approach toward government intervention in declining industries (p.158-159). Why does he support it?
• Describe Ricardo's theory of comparative advantage. Give an example.

⍞ Materials: What Would Keynes Do?, notebook

• DEFINITIONS:
Critical mass is an amount large enough to produce a particular result.

➜ READ
p.165-172

➜ DISCUSS and/or WRITE
• What are the "social costs" of imposing parking charges?

• COMPOSITION - Letter
Write a letter to someone discussing the legalization of drugs. Using your Economics text (and other sources if you'd like), take a stance on the topic and support your position.

⍞ Materials: What Would Keynes Do?, notebook

• DEFINITIONS:
In economics, austerity refers to a government policy of reducing debt and curtailing spending. Think about liquidity as the ability to have cash quickly.

Marginal utility = the economics as a basis for maximization of personal satisfaction/happiness - maximize pleasure and minimize pain (p.19).

➜ READ
p.173-179

➜ DISCUSS and/or WRITE
• Is government debt positive, negative, or in between? Discuss the different economists' views on government debt.
• Contrast the perspectives of Sowell and Pigou on welfare programs.

⍞ Materials: What Would Keynes Do?, notebook

➜ INTRO
For your second reading and moving forward, you will jump back to Chapter 3, "Work," and Chapter 4, "Finance," to finish the Part 2 lessons.

➜ READ
p.180-183; 86-90

➜ DISCUSS and/or WRITE
• Discuss the positions of private and public healthcare options.
• Discuss the theory of human capital as it relates to educational choices.

⍞ Materials: What Would Keynes Do?, notebook

➜ INTRO
Lessons now jump back to Chapter 3, "Work," and Chapter 4, "Finance," to finish the Part 2 lessons.

➜ READ
p.91-98

➜ DISCUSS and/or WRITE
• Reflect on the idea of diminishing marginal utility and happiness economics. Where do you find true happiness?
• Discuss the economic theory "that places emphasis on financial incentives to motivate workers," along with the idea of non-financial incentives shown in Maslow's hierarchy of needs. (p.96-97).

⍞ Materials: What Would Keynes Do?, link, and notebook

• DEFINITIONS:
Libertarianism - a political philosophy that strongly values individual freedom and minimal government involvement

Welfare state - a concept of government in which the state plays a key role in the economic and social well-being of its citizens

➜ RECAP
GDP = Gross Domestic Product, the national income of a country (p.78).

➜ READ
p.99-102

➜ DISCUSS and/ or WRITE
• Discuss the charts on p.101 as they relate to immigration and supply and demand for labor.

➜ INTRO
Entrepreneurship is essential to the economy. Two twentieth-century economists, Joseph Schumpeter and Israel Kirzner, contribute more to the conversation. In Chapter 2, you read how Kirzner suggests that entrepreneurs (and consumers) find ways around problems (p.55), and Schumpeter talks about creative destruction (p.158) - more on that in the finance section. Innovation brings about change, and entrepreneurs are change agents.

In the non-profit sector, innovation is about supporting a mission-driven vision that provides a product or service to supply a need. Think about this in the context of social needs or religious ministry; the organizations that support these areas can be for-profit or non-profit. Non-profit organizations also have business structures. Since the lesson discusses the idea of being employed by a business vs. setting up your own business, now is a good time to take a quick look at what that might mean for an entrepreneur in the ministry sector. Let's take a look at basic finance for nonprofits by watching a short video as an introduction to accounting and business practices.

➜ READ
p.104-107

➜ DISCUSS and/or WRITE
• Discuss the idea of self-employment. Write a brief pro/con list for working for a company vs. being your own boss. What are the "trade-offs"?

➜ VIEW
Watch the section on non-profit accounting. This is a short clip made for a specific software platform, but the fundamental ideas are all explained well.
∞ Video Link: What is Accounting? (Aplos)

⍞ Materials: What Would Keynes Do?, notebook

➜ READ
p.108-115

➜ DISCUSS and/or WRITE
• What are the different potential positive and negative outcomes from each approach to a pay raise?
• What type of boss would you like to have? What type of boss do you think it would make sense to be?

⍞ Materials: What Would Keynes Do?, notebook

➜ READ
p.116-119; p.122-125

➜ DISCUSS and/or WRITE
• Can you think of more pros and cons to maximizing profit vs. focusing on something else as the primary goal?
• Based on the few views presented about how to beat the market, what would you choose to do? Why?

⍞ Materials: What Would Keynes Do?, notebook

➜ READ
p.126-135

➜ DISCUSS and/or WRITE
• What are some factors to consider when threats of high inflation loom on the horizon?
• What are your thoughts about how to make a business more profitable?

⍞ Materials: What Would Keynes Do?, notebook

➜ READ
p.136-147

➜ DISCUSS and/or WRITE
• What are your thoughts on saving? What are some things to consider when trying to avoid financial bubbles? Is any type of debt a good idea? Why or why not?

Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to course.

Questions will come from:
What Would Keynes Do?
assignment_teachersNote:
★ Econ/Civics: Grade 11 (U.S.) — TERM 3 EXAM QUESTIONS
· What Would Keynes Do?: Should the government help failing companies? Give support for and against.
OR
Discuss the income-leisure trade-off (income effect and substitution effect of higher pay). When might you choose to take a promotion? Why might you decline?
OR
Discuss the Lazear quote "Incentives are the essence of economics" in light of Maslow's progressive hierarchy of needs pyramid.
